speaker: interviewee
text: I want to come back, you know, this way we start at the beginning. From the day when we run away from home, you know, and what we saw with our own eyes, that when a person was on the highway, Korets is on the main highway from Moscow to Berlin. The town is divided on the north side and on the south side. In the middle was the main highway, right? Anybody who was lucky, in that they went out on the highway, and they just showed they want to go east, they always took people, you know, by the thousands. The army, you know, when they went back to pick up some-- some-- some bread or something, you know, they took, on the truck, by the hundreds.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians, you know. And as long as you reached to the train, you did not have to buy a ticket. You jumped on the train. You jumped on the engine. You jumped on the platforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember when I was 10 days from Novohrad-Volynskyi to Kiev. Why did it take 10 days? Because the stations, they let through the trains where they were going from the east to the west.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every station, every 15 kilometers, every 20 kilometers, there were special hot water. They called it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Everybody jumped out. And everybody took a  little  bit of hot water--

speaker: interviewer
text: Boiling water to drink, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: To drink or to have tea, you know? And they gave kasha. They gave buckwheat. Anything what they gave for the soldiers, you had a  right to stay in the line. And they gave you with no money.

speaker: interviewer
text: To refugees, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: To refugees. And the Russian people-- I'm talking about the Russian people-- they were so good. They took in us, with both hands. We shared their beds. Actually, they did not have beds. They used to sleep on the oven. You know, the oven from the lime, so on the top was where they were sleeping, right? They took us in over there, with them, together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they used to say, maybe my son is handled the way we are handling this thing, you know? So when they're talking about the Russian system, a bad system. But the people, they were jewels as long as you run away from that bastard, from that Hitler. So as far as I'm concerned, you know, I'm not a communist. I am not a politician. But if somebody is talking about Russia, no Jew has the right to say a bad word, because they handled us like human beings, not like animals. Not like the Poles. Not like the Czech. They were a little bit better than the Poles, you know? But the best nation were the Russians, sharing, you know, helping us with whatnot, with work and with anything that they could. I'm glad you mentioned that. So let's get back to, you were in Bytom, and you're taking a train to Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I told you, we find out that my sister is in Prague. So we took our belongings, we jumped on the train with the child on  our arms. And we came to Prague, my brother-in-law and--

speaker: interviewer
text: So the train was going to Romania, but you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Through Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through Prague.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stepped back down from the train, with a child in   our arms. All the belongings were left on the train, because we didn't have to go-- we didn't have the right to land in Prague, you know. But because the train stopped, so we went out from the train. And my sister grabbed the child, you know. And we went to their place. Then I went, right away, to the Jewish Congress. There was, right there, a Jewish Congress. And I got, right there, a passport, and I got a letter of work. And we were there for two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: From '45 to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To '47. The war was still in Berlin, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you were also driving a truck in Prague?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a taxi.

speaker: interviewer
text: A taxi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. First of all, I was a taxi driver taking tourists from Prague to Theresienstadt. And I saw the ovens. And I saw the chimneys. And I saw the places where the single people were lying in squares. Just they used to crawl in there. I saw the laboratories, bones, blood on the walls. And then they put me to work in a warehouse, in a synagogue, where the synagogue was full with clothes from people. When they brought the clothes from the cleaners, we put the clothes together. We made parcels. And every refugee, when they came to Prague, got a parcel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we used to find little notes. I did find a little ring, but I still have it as of today. I find the patch, Jude. I find teeth in the nylons. What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teeth, golden teeth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Teeth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. Till we got the visa to come to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who-- was somebody from your family in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife's two sisters, they were in Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they sent  you papers to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent a visa, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you arrive here?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came-- we took the bus from Prague through Germany-- but coming to Germany, I was so relieved when I saw the buildings smashed, bombed. I saw the son-of-a-bitches, the Germans, you know, walking around, begging for food. So I was so happy seeing this thing. Till we came to Sweden, to Gothenburg. And we took the boat, to Canada. First, we came to the United States, to New York. And from the United States, we came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived here when?

speaker: interviewee
text: November the 4th, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was life for you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ha, ha, it was a different life here. Ha, ha, it was heaven here. When we stepped in to the boat, we saw the food, with the oranges. But we didn't see this thing for years. So everybody grabbed food. And they got sick. And the first day, first evening, it was all right. But then everybody was sick as the boat came in the middle of the ocean. So there's shaking or there's something, so everybody was  sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you landed in Halifax?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never believed that I'd bring Sonia to New York, because she was so sick. From the minute she stepped in, she was dead. So I asked the people, what can I do? So Sonia said, somehow, if I could get a dill pickle, just you should feel her heart, because no food and no water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the helpers in the boat, they spoke Swedish and English. And I start to work with the hands, because I didn't know the language. So I-- I-- I-- I draw. My habit was drawing, you know? So I made a picture of a cucumber. And I says, I want a cucumber. So they went to the kitchen, and they brought me a cucumber. But a cucumber is-- I want a dill pickle. OK, what's a dill pickle? I didn't know how to explain this thing, you know? And this  was the dream. You know, if she could get a dill pickle, maybe, you know? But somehow, thank God, I brought her through New York. And right away, my uncle and my auntie was waiting for us, because we have some relatives in the States. So then you came to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I came to Winnipeg.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Winnipeg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Winnipeg, we were there, I think, two years, 1947-- three years, two and  a half years. But I remember, the first question was what I never heard in my life. My late brother-in-law asked  me a question. Did you bring some money with you? I said, what money? Who has money? I mean, no. I have nothing. Hey says, too bad. You should tell that you brought 100,000 dollars, you know, so you could show to the Canadian government you made 100,000 dollars, you know. But I couldn't understand this thing till later, a few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what are some highlights about your life in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: No complaints. It's the best country on Earth. We are free. It's heaven here. When I was free. When I could come out, and I wasn't afraid to say that I'm Jewish. And when I could go and work, the same like other people, right? And little by little, little by little, little by little, you know. We were good to Canada with work. And Canada was good   to us, giving us such a free life.

speaker: interviewer
text: I understand, when we talked over coffee, you said you're very connected, still, with Israel and travel there often.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as of today, we are already 54 times in Israel, from 1962. We go twice a year, three times a year, especially when my daughter went to the Jerusalem University. And from the first minute, when I stepped down, I always was thinking how to help Israel and helping in every respect, financially, with good work, with help. I started to deal with Israel, with banks, and I start to build Israel. In Netanya, we had apartments. And anything what a Jew has to do, we have tried to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many children do you have now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we have three daughters, three son-in-laws, 10 grandchildren, three great-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not bad from nothing, from nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, how would you answer a question like, how did the war change you? What-- what did you learn from your experiences in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can't say too much, because I always liked to work from childhood. And I knew, even when I came  to Canada, that without work I will not get no place. I wasn't afraid for anything. The fact is, when I came to Canada, not knowing the language, I was for six months hitting on the books, day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when my late brother-in-law made me as a manager in a building, I did not know the word 'manager.' So I says, what is manager? He did not explain to  me, never. But living here, I saw what manager means. And then he put me in a factory. I should  work, because he was manufacturing clothes. So my mind was occupied with who knows what. So I put my finger underneath, you know? And the needle went in through my nail. So then I became a merchant. I went to a factory, and I bought remnants for 200 dollars. It was a pile. So we took these pieces of remnants. We rent a store, with the help of my brother-in-law. And we put the pieces together, put the pieces on the shelves. And the store was opposite a farmer's market. And on the market used to come Polish people, Ukrainian, Czech people, all kind of nationalities. So when a lady comes in, she wants a piece of cotton. We didn't know the difference between cotton and gabardine and other-- we were never in this line. But we learned a few words, just go and find in the shelves. If you see a piece of material, come over. So she brings a piece of material, and she tells me, this is cotton. So I marked down  in Yiddish, 'cotton.' How much it is? I said, give me 20 cents, 25 cents, 30 cents. Anyways, to make the story short, we were in this store for one year. And we made 5,000 dollars a year. This was 5 million dollars. And from then, we changed the store to a department store. I bought odds and ends, different sizes, broken sizes. Then I sold this thing. And the Red River in Winnipeg was flooded, you know? So we left to Regina. Coming to Regina, what can you do? So we became-- my late brother-- well, my sister's husband, he was a professional butcher from home. So he became the kosher butcher in Regina. You did?

speaker: interviewee
text: He became, with my help. I had saved up a few hundred dollars, so I gave it to him. And he opened the kosher butcher shop. Of course, our expenses was 150 dollars a week. Yet we took in only 70 dollars a week. So we have to bring from our pocket what we didn't have, about 40, 50 dollars, you know. We saw the time is bad. So we decided to buy a little truck, and we'll go out to the country. We will buy eggs and chicken and this  and this and  little  of this. And sure enough, I took the truck, because, for me, driving a truck is nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I come to a farmer. And I ask him, do you have something to sell? He says, yes, I have a cow with a broken leg. How  much do you want for the cow? 40 dollars. I say, I'll give 40 dollars. I didn't know the difference between a cow and between a steer, between a bull. I didn't know the difference. But a cow? Let  him buy a cow. So I'll give you 40 dollars, but you have to help me, because you have to help me to put the cow on the truck. Because the cow was lying on the floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways, I give him the 40 dollars, cash. He lifted the cow on a chain. I backed up the truck. I took the cow to the packing house, to Burns in Regina. And I sold the cow for 60 dollars. I was thinking, if for one cow, I made 20 dollars, on 10 cows, I'll make 200 dollars.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I start to rattle, every day, day and night, day and night, day and night, day and night, for about eight years, until my back gave up pulling a cow on the truck. Why did I pull  the cow on the truck? Because a farmer wants to get rid of a wild cow. The cow was worth-- I knew already. I was already a professional man with cattle. So I thought that the cow is worth 130 dollars. How  much do you want for the cow? He says, give me 100 dollars. Gee whiz, we shall make 30 dollars on one cow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyways, we start to pull this cow on the truck, and I broke my back. And as of today, I suffer. In fact, I'll have to be now operated with my back. And for seven years, we were going day and night, day  and night. We did flood the market with cattle. And beside this thing, we made a few dollars. We bought an old house. My wife fixed up the house. She was renting the house to girls-- mostly girls-- two girls in a room. We had a two-burner, one bathtub, and one toilet. So in the morning, people were standing in line. And the children, they had to bathe. And they have to go to the washroom after the girls would go away to work. So we always were dreaming, if we would have a house with two washrooms, we'll be the luckiest people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we have eight washrooms in the house. Tell me, Mr. Tessler, obviously, you're happy with your life and with your lovely family and lovely home. And-- and you had some good experiences during the war, also. Why did you decide to make this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: This tape? I think that everybody, everybody who live through, they should come for this thing. Because let the world know what these son-of-a-bitches did  to a nation, to innocent people. Innocent for nothing. For nothing, you know? To take babies?

speaker: interviewer
text: My sister, Selma, she will tell you, that in 1942, when they start to chase the Jews to the ghettos, a Nazi was chasing, was behind a woman with a gun aimed on her. And she had a baby  in her arms. The baby started to cry. So that son-of-a-bitch shot the girl. And he said, now, you'll go to the death with more happiness. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I would like to go over to the-- I wasn't in my town up till 1989. I was always thinking, if I'll come and see that disaster of a town, I'll collapse. But one nice day, I decided I must go. So in 1989, I took two daughters--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: --two son-in-laws, and me. And we decided to go to the little town of Korets from Canada. As you know, maybe, all of you who know, up till two years ago, up till 1991 or '92, you couldn't travel in Russia. Only if you have a direction to go to Moscow, you're allowed to go to Moscow. But you couldn't go to small, little towns. But knowing the language, I just thought that I'm going. I find out, from a friend of mine, a name  in Kiev of a friend. So we come to Kiev, 2 o'clock at night. I'm 250 kilometers away from my town. Of course, on the road, they asked us-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: I jumped ahead of the time where the police stopped  us. Where are we going? I says, where is the street, you know, Korets? And they started in speaking Russian. They didn't say nothing. Anyways, I came to Russia, to Kiev. 2   o'clock, I call up this guy. I says, sir, I'll pay as much as you want. Take me to my little town, Korets. Korets was 250 kilometers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, he took us. And when I came, I saw three massive graves, covered up with an inch or two inches of sand, and the bones were sticking out from the thing. And when I said kaddish, I said, I must bring this terrible place-- that people should rest like human beings not like animals. Of course, from there, I went to Israel. And I told my landsleit we have in Israel, about 300 or 400 people, but they ran away to Chelyabinsk. Because I told you, before, that a group was mobilized to the east. And I told them this thing. Anyway, since then, I organized. And we built a cemetery, something special. What I did, having experience in the building business, the bones were sticking out from the little hill. So I made a big retaining wall, about two-feet high. We filled up the retaining wall with ground. We covered up the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we put three matzevahs, marble matzevahs. Tombstones, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we made a matzevah. We made beautiful--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: We made beautiful these three matzevahs, beautiful gates. In fact, I have some pictures. And I'll show those to you later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I can be proud of this thing, because nobody in Ukraine even-- I don't  know why I was in Poland. That was another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on, finish your sentence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So, I-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Just a moment. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I think if we show your pictures, now, you can tell us more about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait, OK- OK, yeah. No, OK, OK. Now in 1994, last year, we made unveiling on this thing. We had 45-- we had 50 people from Israel and from Canada. And the Ukrainian government announced to the neighbors, and we had about 2,000 Ukrainian women with children, with a priest. And they opened the three matzevahs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the most tragic minute in our life--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to see this cemetery, to see the bones from children, the elbows, their finger, the scalp holes. And then we are doing, now, the old cemetery. If somebody died in 1941, they threw the people as garbage on this old cemetery. Of course, during the 50 years, the fence of the cemetery was broken. The bones are still in the ground. So now, what I'm doing, I'm putting up a fence. I made a deal with a contractor for 25,000 dollars. And he's making a big fence. This old cemetery is about 25 acres' land.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're putting a fence, and we're putting frames. And the government promised me, and I'm going to pay them, every year, 120 dollars, they should take care. And they put signs that nobody should touch these holy places. OK, Mr. Tessler, thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it? This picture is from my family taken in 1936. From the left side, it's me. The second one is my sister, Faiga, then my sister Zlata, and then my brother, Shlomo. And the middle is Suri Malka, my mother. But she died in 1942. This is my driver's license that I received in Stalingrad a month after the war broke out, because I destroyed my driver's license and my passport on the way running to Stalingrad. I was afraid then the Germans should  not catch me, and they should see that I'm Jewish. This is a picture taken in 1994, when I was unveiling at the cemetery where I made it. I covered up 5,000 Jews slaughtered by the Germans. And I made a beautiful [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], a cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's in Kozak, three kilometers from Korets. This is my house, where I was born and raised. This picture was taken in 1994, when I made the unveiling at the cemetery Kozak. I'm in front of the house with my granddaughter, Suri Feinstein, from Dallas, Texas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this the house in Korets?

speaker: interviewee
text: This house  is in Korets. And it, as  of today, still stands. This picture was taken in 1988 at the state of [INAUDIBLE] campaign in our house. [INAUDIBLE] Rabin attended with us. This picture was taken in 1991 where Sonia and me bought the cafe in the Baycrest, with all the family. Now this picture is taken by us in Jerusalem. It's called 'The Valley of Destroyed Communities.' And in the front, is the menorah given to us for our big pledge to Yad Vashem. This picture was taken by me in 1994, August the 7th, at the unveiling of our beloved ones where they were slaughtered by the Germans and the Ukrainians in 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which place is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kozak, three kilometers from Korets. On my left is my darling, Sonia, my wife. In the back is Celia and Debbie. And it's too bad we couldn't have Reesa. Reesa is in Dallas, Texas.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Debbie. I'm the youngest of the three daughters. And though there's only four of us here today, thank God, we have a very rich family. We just counted 19 of us. And from our mom and our dad, who narrowly survived, there's a huge tree now with 19 and growing. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] There's grandchildren and great-grandchildren and spouses and grand-spouses. And we're very proud of our parents. We're proud of their strengths. We're proud that they survived. And we hope to be able to continue, in our lives and passing on to our children and our grandchildren, the commitment to Judaism and to our heritage that we learned from Mom and Dad's survival.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm Celia. And I am the eldest. I was born in Tashkent. I'm sure that  my father has mentioned it before. As a little baby, I often wonder what it was like for my parents to have gone through the Holocaust with me. When I look at my little grandson, who is three years old and is as rambunctious as can be, I think how difficult it must've been for me and for my parents to have survived those terrible first years. But amazingly, we have survived. And we've gone on. And we continue to go on. We are pleased that we have our parents with us, our grandparents-- which is something we never had. And now, they are great-grandparents. And we are glad.
